note: Mathlib/NumberTheory/ArithmeticFunction/Misc.lean:259-267 — Ω(n), notated `Ω` (scoped ArithmeticFunction.Omega), = n.primeFactorsList.length, i.e. number of prime factors with multiplicity; this is the 'big omega' half of the Wikipedia 'Prime omega function' article.
verify_note: decl confirmed at cited lines (def cardFactors 259-260, cardFactors_apply theorem 267) with scoped notation Ω; Wikidata Q55630466 (enwiki 'Prime omega function') covers both Ω and ω jointly and there is no separate QID for either half, so per-function exact edges are the correct modeling and the note is transparent about covering only the Ω half.

note: Mathlib/NumberTheory/ArithmeticFunction/Misc.lean:324-328 — ω(n), notated `ω` (scoped ArithmeticFunction.omega), = n.primeFactorsList.dedup.length, i.e. number of distinct prime factors; the 'little omega' half of the same Wikipedia article.
verify_note: decl confirmed at cited lines (def cardDistinctFactors 324-325, scoped notation 327-328); Wikidata search turns up no distinct QID for 'little omega function' alone, confirming Q55630466 is the correct shared anchor and cardDistinctFactors is precisely ω(n).

note: Mathlib/GroupTheory/SpecificGroups/Dihedral.lean:33 — `inductive DihedralGroup (n : ℕ) : Type`, the dihedral group of order 2n (r i / sr i constructors); direct formalization of the concept.
verify_note: inductive DihedralGroup at line 33 confirmed verbatim; Wikidata Q558339 label 'dihedral group' / enwiki 'Dihedral group' matches exactly, decl is the canonical Mathlib formalization.

note: Mathlib/Combinatorics/SimpleGraph/Diam.lean:434 — `def center (G : SimpleGraph α) : Set α` = set of vertices with eccentricity equal to the graph's radius; exactly the graph-theoretic 'center' concept.
verify_note: def center at line 434 confirmed, {u | G.eccent u = G.radius}; Wikidata Q5597075 description 'set of all vertices of minimum eccentricity' is definitionally equivalent (radius = min eccentricity), exact match.

note: Mathlib/Combinatorics/Additive/Corner/Roth.lean:164 — 'Roth's theorem for ℕ': a subset of {0,…,n-1} of density ≥ ε contains a nontrivial 3-term AP once n is large enough; the classical Roth's theorem on 3-APs.
verify_note: theorem at line 164 confirmed, docstring itself is titled '**Roth's theorem** for ℕ'; Wikidata Q56313802 enwiki title 'Roth's theorem on arithmetic progressions' matches exactly, quantitative-density statement is the standard formulation.
